comments.text: On page 170 of [Er80], Erdos states he can only do this for $\epsilon = 1/2$. But in this paper, in Theorem 1, he provides a proof for the problem with the bound of $n^{1/2-\epsilon}$ and conjectures that it probably remains true if you replace $n^{1/2-\epsilon}$ with $n^{1-\epsilon}$, which is the problem above. This makes it sound like in [Er80], he made have made a mistake and meant he could prove this for the bound of $n^{1/2-\epsilon}$ and not $\epsilon=1/2$?
This can be deduced from Matomäki–Radziwiłł theorem on multiplicative functions with a fairly short argument. The note written by GPT-5.5 Pro is here. I haven't found this observation in literature.

Using known results for pair correlations between $P(n+i)$ and $P(n+j)$, e.g., from this paper of Teravainen, one should be able to prove a significantly stronger result, namely that for any $k$, one has $P(n \dots(n+k)) = n^{(\lambda + o_{k \to \infty}(1))k}$ on a set of logarithmic density $1 - o_{k \to \infty}(1)$, where $\lambda = 0.6243\dots$ is the Golomb-Dickman constant. EDIT: Actually, this result doesn't quite strictly contain #1201 because it involves logarithmic density instead of natural density. On the other hand, it should be noted that Theorem 1.19 of Teravainen's paper already establishes $P(n (n+1)) > n^{2-\varepsilon}$ on a set of positive lower natural density. SECOND EDIT: As pointed out below, the above comments actually apply to $P(n) \dots P(n+k)$ and $P(n) P(n+1)$ instead of $P(n \dots (n+k))$ and $P(n (n+1))$ respectively.
The bound you state applies to $P(n)P(n+1)\dots P(n+k)$, not $P(n(n+1)\dots (n+k))$.

Oops, you are right, thanks! So instead what Teravainen's results give is that $\asymp \varepsilon k$ of the $P(n),\dots,P(n+k)$ are of size at least $n^{1-\varepsilon}$ for $n$ in a set of logarithmic density at least $1-\varepsilon$, but this is overkill for this problem. (I was wondering why Erdos stated a result that seemed far weaker than what ought to be true, but it turns out that I misread the question, or more precisely I assumed incorrectly that $P$ was essentially multiplicative.) On the other hand, #1201 remains technically open because it was not established that the natural density of the set $\{ n: P(n \dots (n+k)) \geq n^{1-\varepsilon}\}$ actually exists; instead, it is shown that the natural lower density is at least $1-\eta$.

Thank you for pointing it out - it seems that proving the existence of the natural density in this case is quite hard. I couldn't really complete it in general though I've got\[ \#\{n\le N:P ^{+}((n)\cdots(n+h-1))\le n^\alpha\} \sim \rho(1/\alpha)^h N . \]for $1/2 < \alpha < 1$. This is related to your work with Teräväinen, work of Wang and a couple of other papers. Here's the note by GPT-5.5 Pro with these results and explicit conjecture that's missing to prove the natural density exists.

I do think it's reasonable to read "density at least" as "lower density at least" and "density at most" as "upper density at most" rather than requiring a proof that density exists (since the phenomenon of proving the density to exist being harder than proving an upper bound for the upper density or lower bound for the lower density is a rather general one).

I agree; I think this is generally how Erdős used these terms. (When he was specifically curious about the existence of the density he was generally clear about this.) I've tried to update all the problem descriptions to reflect this, but missed this one.

It should be stressed that that the results on that note are conditional on an unproven (and difficult) hypothesis (which the AI calls $LPD(\alpha,h)$). I would appreciate it if these rather important caveats are included in the human summary of AI-generated arguments going forward.
Yes, the note I shared in the comments (not the original one) is mostly about proving that natural density exists and it is conditional, and conditional on a pretty difficult hypothesis.
Congrats. Standard check found no issues.
